summary: Graham reports the first Summer Founders Program exceeded expectations, with several of the eight funded startups appearing likely to succeed despite typical low startup success rates. He notes strong follow-on funding and acquisition interest, and wonders whether the applicant pool was unusually strong, though he sees no clear anomaly yet.
key_points: The program performed better than expected given typical startup success rates.
Several startups seem likely to succeed and have strong investor or acquirer interest.
Graham questions whether the cohort’s strength reflects an anomaly, but is unsure.
money_quotes: Overall only about 10% of startups succeed, but if I had to guess now, I'd predict three or four of the eight startups we funded will make it.
Of the startups that needed further funding, I believe all have either closed a round or are likely to soon.
Two have already turned down (lowball) acquisition offers.
We would have been happy if just one of the eight seemed promising by the end of the summer.
Did some kind of anomaly make this summer's applicants especially good?
key_insight: The first cohort’s performance suggests the program may consistently identify unusually strong startups, though it’s too early to tell whether this was a one-off.
